segments.title: A battlecruiser reserve at Singapore
segments.summary: The campaign review finds four battlecruisers and two light cruisers at Singapore, illustrating how a distant squadron can become an operational reserve.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke takes stock of heavy ships already based at Singapore while deciding where to move forces. The moment is useful for thinking about forward basing: battlecruisers supply striking power, while light cruisers give a detached force more scouting and screening options.

evidence.note: He identifies the ships at Singapore as four battlecruisers and two light cruisers.

segments.title: Crew capacity before expanding the submarine force
segments.summary: Maximum crew training and transport capacity underpin a decision to expand the submarine arm rather than treating new hulls as cost-free additions.
segments.body: The campaign screen makes personnel generation, training, transport capacity, research funding, and shipbuilding compete in the same budgetary picture. Dr. Clarke then orders more coastal submarines, showing why a navy's manpower and support capacity matter alongside the number of ships built.

evidence.note: He reviews maximum crew training and transport capacity before ordering coastal submarines.

segments.title: Screening and scouting ahead of the battle line
segments.summary: Light cruisers and destroyers are assigned to screen, scout, and hunt while the capital ships form the battle line behind them.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke explicitly sets a light cruiser to screen and scout for the battle line, then detaches other ships to search. The resulting task force of a capital ship, three light cruisers, and roughly nine destroyers demonstrates the layered jobs of scouts, escorts, and heavier striking units.

evidence.note: He assigns screening and scouting orders and describes the detached task force.

segments.title: Why fleet speed has to match the force
segments.summary: A fast battlecruiser is grouped with ships limited to 24 knots, highlighting the practical cost of mixing unequal speeds in one formation.
segments.body: The commentary compares a 30-knot ship with older battlecruisers that are now roughly as fast as newer battleships. A task force is constrained by the ships it must keep together, so speed matching affects whether a fast unit can exploit its mobility or must slow to the formation's pace.

evidence.note: He compares the speed of Conqueror and older battlecruisers with the formation's 24-knot limit.

segments.title: Secondary batteries create a crossfire problem
segments.summary: A French battlecruiser is caught under concentrated six-inch fire, showing how numerous secondary guns can punish a lighter opponent at close range.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke notes that the opposing ship has fifteen 15-inch guns but faces a battlecruiser with a dense secondary battery as well as heavy guns. The encounter stresses that a broadside is more than its biggest caliber: secondary guns can shape close-range engagements and make a crossfire dangerous even for a capital ship.

evidence.note: He describes the six-inch crossfire and compares the opposing heavy-gun batteries.

segments.title: Range, speed, and armor in a battlecruiser duel
segments.summary: The engagement weighs gun range, best-speed accuracy, ammunition, and armor before deciding how much risk is acceptable against a tougher opponent.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke holds his ship near the edge of its 21-kilometre gun range and keeps 25 knots as the preferred speed for accuracy. He argues against closing with a battleship whose range and armor are at least as good, then reassesses when ammunition runs low and the enemy's thick armor absorbs punishment. The sequence is a compact illustration of how range, speed, protection, and ammunition impose limits on tactical freedom.

evidence.note: He compares gun ranges and speed, then discusses armor, ammunition, and the risks of continuing the fight.

segments.title: Why a distant-station cruiser needs torpedoes
segments.summary: A cruiser operating away from battleship support is judged to need torpedoes as protection and a means of creating a close-range threat.
segments.body: Watching an enemy cruiser stand off at range, Dr. Clarke says he would instead close for torpedo attacks if the ship carried them. His point is about the vulnerability of cruisers on distant stations: without nearby capital ships, a torpedo armament gives a lighter vessel a way to complicate an enemy's choices.

evidence.note: He explains why a cruiser away from battleship cover should have torpedoes.

segments.title: Bulkheads improve survival but cost weight
segments.summary: Maximum reinforced bulkheads help contain flooding and fire, but their weight reduces the margin available for fuel and other design choices.
segments.body: The design discussion treats internal subdivision as a genuine survivability benefit rather than a free upgrade. Reinforced bulkheads improve resistance to fire and flooding, but the added hull weight has to be balanced against endurance and other requirements.

evidence.note: He describes reinforced bulkheads as valuable for fire and flooding control but costly in hull weight.

segments.title: Concentrating fleets for an invasion of northern France
segments.summary: The campaign shifts fleets toward northern France, illustrating how an invasion plan absorbs available ships, bases, crews, and older hulls.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke opens another naval-invasion option and repeatedly moves ships north to improve the chance of success. He also reviews ships in repair, commissions available units, and scraps older vessels. The watch point shows how amphibious pressure depends on concentrating enough usable force in the right theatre, not simply on total tonnage on the map.

evidence.note: He plans invasions and moves fleets and available ships toward northern France.

segments.title: A capital ship designed to escort itself
segments.summary: The design idea is a powerful ship whose six-inch battery can handle destroyers and light cruisers, reducing its dependence on close escort support.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke considers whether a sufficiently powerful ship can help protect itself from smaller cruisers and destroyers. The thought experiment highlights a recurring force-design tradeoff: more secondary armament can improve self-defence, but those guns consume weight and space that could serve speed, armor, range, or a dedicated escort force.

evidence.note: He considers a powerful ship using six-inch guns to deal with destroyers and light cruisers.

segments.title: Balancing a super-battleship design
segments.summary: A super-battleship design is reduced from an extravagant starting point by trading speed, hull protection, armament layout, and weight balance.
segments.body: The design starts at 104,000 tons and is repeatedly adjusted: speed falls from 33 to 30 knots, hull and torpedo protection are set, and pitch, roll, and weight offsets are monitored. Dr. Clarke compares 16.5-inch and larger guns, considers turret placement, and ultimately chooses a heavily protected but balanced arrangement. It is a useful look at naval architecture as a sequence of linked compromises rather than a search for the largest possible statistic.

evidence.note: He adjusts speed, protection, armament, stability, and weight while designing a super-battleship.

segments.title: The Hood class: broadside and fire arcs
segments.summary: The completed Hood-class design combines nine 16.5-inch guns with an eighteen-gun six-inch broadside and considers how many secondary guns cover each end of the ship.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke names the new generation of battleship after Hood and inventories its main and secondary batteries. He distinguishes broadside firepower from the guns that can cover forward and astern arcs, a reminder that a ship's tactical usefulness depends on gun placement as well as total gun count.

evidence.note: He gives the Hood-class main and secondary armament and describes its forward and stern coverage.

segments.title: Do not open a gap in a convoy screen
segments.summary: A convoy escort that charges out after an attacker can leave the merchant ships exposed, giving a fast surface raider a route through the screen.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke deliberately drives for the merchants and uses the outcome to explain a convoy-protection failure. Escorts must prevent an attacker from exploiting an opening; pursuing too aggressively can abandon the cargo ships they were meant to shield. He also notes that an attacking surface raider must still get clear without taking prohibitive damage.

evidence.note: He explains how an escort charging an attacker can leave a gap that exposes the convoy.

segments.title: Togo faced criticism before Tsushima
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke recalls that Admiral Togo faced pressure before Tsushima because the Russian fleet had not yet come out to be defeated.
segments.body: The short historical aside places Togo's later reputation in a less certain pre-battle context. Dr. Clarke notes that critics viewed the preceding campaign as ineffective while the Russian fleet remained in harbour, and that there were even calls for Togo to resign or commit seppuku. It is a useful reminder that famous commanders were not always judged as inevitable victors before their decisive battle.

evidence.note: He says that Togo was criticised before Tsushima because the Russian fleet had not left harbour.

segments.title: Using light cruisers to torpedo a heavy cruiser
segments.summary: A light-cruiser force closes on a heavier cruiser to launch torpedoes before its seven-inch guns can exploit the lighter ships' thin armor.
segments.body: Facing a heavy cruiser, a light cruiser, and destroyers, Dr. Clarke identifies the heavy cruiser as the immediate threat and plans to get torpedoes into it on the first pass. The timing sequence includes turning to avoid incoming torpedoes and then pressing closer so the target cannot evade. It illustrates how torpedoes can let lighter ships threaten a heavier gun platform, but only if the approach is coordinated and survivable.

evidence.note: He plans a first-run torpedo attack on the heavy cruiser and describes the approach and evasion timing.

segments.title: Merchant ships can still carry a sting
segments.summary: A seemingly undefended merchant convoy reveals torpedo capability, prompting a comparison with armed merchant cruisers.
segments.body: After finding no warships in the convoy, Dr. Clarke discovers that merchant ships can still launch torpedoes. He connects the surprise to armed merchant cruisers and notes that adding torpedo weapons would make them far more dangerous to an attacker. The moment is a reminder not to treat merchant traffic as tactically inert simply because it lacks a visible escort.

evidence.note: He discovers that merchant ships have torpedoes and compares the idea with armed merchant cruisers.

segments.title: Two ways to evade a torpedo
segments.summary: A torpedo attack prompts a concise contrast between turning away to outrun it and turning toward it to dodge across its path.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke describes two basic evasive choices: turn away and attempt to run clear, or turn toward the torpedo to evade it. The right choice depends on relative speed, geometry, and the time available, but the distinction is a useful starting point for reading close-range torpedo manoeuvres.

evidence.note: He contrasts turning away to outrun a torpedo with turning toward it to dodge.
